intro.value: This worksheet compresses the full Eq. 1 through Eq. 16 arc into one roadmap. It is meant to answer three questions at once: what each stage accomplishes, what the big picture conclusion is, and when each equation family is the right tool.

Read it as a navigation layer, not as a substitute for the detailed derivations. The left side explains what each stage does; the right side shows the representative equations that belong to that stage.

content.value: Eq. 1 starts at the microscopic level: each oscillator has its own phase theta sub i and natural frequency omega sub i, so the raw problem is many coupled variables.

Eq. 2 is the phase-reduction move. It replaces detailed limit-cycle dynamics by phase dynamics, which is the right approximation when coupling is weak and the oscillators stay near the same underlying cycle.

Eq. 3 is the concrete Kuramoto mean-field model, and Eq. 4 rewrites the same interaction in the co-rotating mean-field picture where the collective pull K r becomes explicit. That is the point where locked-versus-drifting behavior first becomes visible.

Use Eq. 1-4 when you are setting up the model, justifying the Kuramoto approximation, or running direct phase simulations. Big takeaway: the many-body dynamics become analytically tractable only after they are reduced to phases plus one collective field.

reference.referenceLabel: Open the canonical Kuramoto model topic

content.value: Eq. 5 defines the complex order parameter r e to the i psi. It compresses the whole phase population into two macroscopic numbers: r for coherence and psi for the average phase.
This is the first major conceptual reduction. Instead of tracking every oscillator separately, the analysis now asks how the population feeds back onto its own centroid.

Use Eq. 5 whenever you need a quantitative measure of synchronization rather than raw individual phases. It is the correct starting point for every later self-consistency calculation, because all of the later equations are ultimately solving for r.
Eq. 6 moves from sums over individual oscillators to a density description. That is the large-population viewpoint.
Eq. 7 is the continuity or transport equation for that density. Its job is conservation: oscillators move around the phase circle, but they are neither created nor destroyed.
Eq. 8 specializes this transport story to stationary or co-rotating states. That is the preparation step that makes a time-independent partially synchronized population describable at all.

Use Eq. 6-8 when N is large and you want densities instead of individual trajectories. Big takeaway: the continuum limit is what turns synchronization into an integral problem rather than a huge coupled system.

reference.referenceLabel: Open the stationary-density topic that this machinery feeds into

content.value: Eq. 9 makes the stationary population split explicit: one branch is locked and one branch is drifting. That is the point where the population-level geometry becomes concrete.
Eq. 10 substitutes that stationary density back into the order-parameter definition. The result is the full self-consistency equation for r, still carrying both locked and drifting contributions.

Eq. 11 is the cleaner reduced form obtained after symmetry removes the net drifting contribution and the locked part is rewritten in the phase variable. From here on, the onset analysis becomes much simpler.

Use Eq. 9 when you need the explicit stationary split, Eq. 10 for the full self-consistency story, and Eq. 11 for the reduced threshold analysis. Big takeaway: synchronization becomes a fixed-point problem once r appears on both sides of the equation.

reference.value: Eq. 10 is the substitution step between these two forms.

content.value: Eq. 12 comes from the nonzero synchronized branch of Eq. 11. It is not the whole solution set; it is the equation satisfied by the branch with r greater than zero.
Its point is to locate onset. By taking the limit r goes to zero from the positive side, Eq. 12 yields the general threshold formula in terms of g evaluated at zero.

Use Eq. 12 when you want the threshold for a general smooth even frequency distribution. Big takeaway: before choosing any special distribution, the onset threshold is already determined by the density at the center.
Eq. 13 is not another self-consistency equation. It is a choice of frequency distribution: a Lorentzian centered at zero with width parameter gamma.

Its role is to turn the general threshold and self-consistency formulas into an exactly solvable example. Once this g of omega is substituted into Eq. 12 and Eq. 11, the integrals can be carried out explicitly.
Use Eq. 13 when you want an explicit solvable distribution and a closed-form synchronized branch. Big takeaway: this is a special exact example, not the universal answer for arbitrary g.

Eq. 14 gives the universal square-root scaling of the synchronized branch near threshold. Its point is not exactness away from K sub c. Its point is the leading-order shape of the bifurcation right as synchronization sets in.

Eq. 15 defines asymptotic equivalence. That is what tells you how to read the tilde symbol in Eq. 14: the compared expressions have the same leading behavior as the control parameter approaches threshold.
Eq. 16 generalizes the same idea from one comparison to a whole asymptotic series. It explains why later derivations keep only the first few powers that actually control the onset scaling.

Use Eq. 14 when K is close to K sub c and you need the leading-order bifurcation law. Use Eq. 15 and Eq. 16 when you need to interpret what that approximation means and what higher-order corrections would look like.

The Eq. 1 through Eq. 16 chain has one main narrative: start with many coupled oscillators, compress them into a collective order parameter, derive a stationary self-consistency equation, find the threshold where the nonzero branch appears, then separate exact special cases from universal near-threshold behavior.

The most important conceptual jump is from microscopic dynamics to self-consistency. Once r appears on both sides of the equation, synchronization becomes a fixed-point problem rather than just a simulation result.
The second major jump is from exact formulas to asymptotic interpretation. The Lorentzian branch is exact for one chosen g, whereas Eq. 14-16 tell you what survives generically near onset.

Scenario map: use Eq. 1-4 for model setup or simulation, Eq. 5 for measuring coherence, Eq. 6-9 for continuum stationary reasoning, Eq. 10-12 for implicit self-consistency and threshold, Eq. 13 for an explicit solvable example, and Eq. 14-16 for the universal onset law.
